case_name: Kunkel, A. v. Abington Memorial Hospital

summary.brief: Kunkel, A. v. Abington Memorial Hospital. Medical malpractice case involving paediatrics.

evidence_index.text: Kunkel received emergency medical care from Abington Memorial Hospital (AMH); Francesca Delach, M.D.; and Ryan Shadis, M.D. (collectively, Appellees) after being badly injured in an automobile accident.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: During the first two days of his hospital stay, the contact lenses Kunkel had been wearing remained on his eyes.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: A jury found Appellees’ conduct to be negligent, but not the cause of Kunkel’s asserted injuries.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: It is my considered medical opinion that the failure to recognize the presence of Mr. Kunkel’s contact lenses compounded by the failure to remove the contact lenses in a timely manner constituted medical malpractice.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. James Aquavella

evidence_index.text: In my opinion, the failure to timely identify and remove his contact lenses ultimately caused him to develop bilateral corneal scarring.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. James Aquavella

evidence_index.text: Despite regular examinations of Kunkel’s pupils in the first two days of his admission, the medical staff did not discover that Kunkel had been wearing contact lenses on both of his eyes.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Alexander never received an ophthalmology consult nor antibiotic eyedrops while at AMH based on my review of the medical records.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. James Aquavella

evidence_index.text: The trial court acted within its discretion by precluding them from testifying on the cause of Kunkel’s asserted eye injuries.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: The first time either of the physicians specifically linked Kunkel’s corneal injuries to contact lenses came in their deposition testimony.
evidence_index.speaker: Court reasoning

evidence_index.text: Kunkel was transported to AMH, where he was intubated and unconscious for about three days.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

initial_state.chief_complaint: Unconscious after being ejected from a vehicle in a rollover accident
initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient was involved in a serious automobile accident, ejected from the vehicle, and sustained numerous catastrophic injuries.
initial_state.initial_working_problem: Severe trauma with potential ocular involvement
critical_findings: Contact lenses remained in eyes for two days

requestables.name: Ophthalmology Consult
requestables.description: Consultation to assess potential ocular injuries

reveal.result_summary: Not performed during initial hospital stay
reveal.clinical_significance: Missed opportunity to identify and treat corneal issues
reveal.decision_impact: Could have led to earlier intervention and prevention of complications

requestables.name: Topical Antibiotic Administration
requestables.description: Application of antibiotic eyedrops for corneal protection

reveal.decision_impact: Contributed to development of corneal ulcers

timeline_phases.name: Emergency Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents to AMH after a serious accident

timeline_phases.name: Initial Hospital Stay
timeline_phases.description: Patient remains at AMH with contact lenses in place

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient admitted to AMH with contact lenses in place
decision_points.prompt: What should be done regarding the patient's contact lenses?

options.description: Remove contact lenses immediately and consult ophthalmology
options.clinical_reasoning: Standard care to prevent corneal damage and infection

options.description: Leave contact lenses in place and monitor
options.clinical_reasoning: Negligent as it increases risk of infection

actual_action_defendant.description: Contact lenses were left in place for two days

expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert evidence on standard care for contact lens wearers

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to remove contact lenses led to corneal ulcers
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Immediate removal and treatment with antibiotics
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Standard of care for trauma patients with contact lenses

patient_outcome.description: Patient developed bilateral corneal ulcers and permanent scarring

key_findings: Jury did not find negligence to be the cause of eye injuries

malpractice_determination.point_of_failure: Failure to remove contact lenses and provide timely ophthalmology consult
malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Timely removal and treatment could have prevented corneal damage

factual_timeline.event: Patient admitted to AMH after accident

allegations.allegation: Failure to remove contact lenses and provide timely treatment

court_findings.legal_significance: Jury did not find negligence to be the cause of injuries
